repository.corpname: M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections & Archives

did.abstract: The Capital Jury Project (CJP) was started in 1991 under the direction of principal investigator, William J. Bowers. The project was funded by a NSF grant. There are three phases to the project.
did.physloc: The materials are located onsite in the department.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Processed in 2015 M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections and Archives staff.

accessrestrict.head: Access
accessrestrict.p: To respect and fulfill the guidelines of the original National Science Foundation grant which supported the Capital Jury Project, all of sub-series 1.1, 1.3, 3.1, and 3.3 are currently closed to researchers. The materials will be open 75 years after the date of their respective creation. Sub-series 1.1, 1.3, 3.1 and 3.3 will be fully open in 2086 to account for undated material in each sub-series. Archivists also separated and closed portions of a handful of files in subseries 1.2 and 3.2, and series 2 and 5 to fulfill the guidelines of the original NSF grant. The date the separated material will open to researchers is noted within each respective file.
All other material in the collection are open and unrestricted.

As an alternative to accessing the fully closed sub-series, researchers may also be able to access portions of aggregate data from the CJP study, which is not part of the collection at the University at Albany, to test specific research questions. Please contact an archivist for more details.

userestrict.head: Copyright
userestrict.p: This page may contain links to digital objects. Access to these images and the technical capacity to download them does not imply permission for re-use. Digital objects may be used freely for personal reference use, referred to, or linked to from other web sites.
Researchers do not have permission to publish or disseminate material from these collections without permission from an archivist and/or the copyright holder.

The researcher assumes full responsibility for conforming to the laws of copyright. Some materials in these collections may be protected by the U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) and/or by the copyright or neighboring-rights laws of other nations. More information about U.S. Copyright is provided by the Copyright Office. Additionally, re-use may be restricted by terms of University Libraries gift or purchase agreements, donor restrictions, privacy and publicity rights, licensing and trademarks.
The Department of Special Collections and Archives is eager to hear from any copyright owners who are not properly identified so that appropriate information may be provided in the future.

acqinfo.head: Acquisition Information
acqinfo.p: All items in this collection were transferred to the University Libraries, M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections and Archives in 2005, 2014, and 2015 by members of the Capital Jury Project at Norteastern and the University at Albany. In 2017, Deborah Good, former spouse of Bill Bowers, donated a small amount of additional correspondence, reports, data studies, and administrative files that have not been interfiled.

bioghist.head: Historical Note
bioghist.p: The Capital Jury Project was initiated in 1991 by a consortium of university-based researchers with support from the National Science Foundation. The Project was administered nationally by Dr. William Bowers, principal research scientist, Northeastern University.

Phase I of the Project covered 353 capital trials in 15 states. The Project continued with the Capital Jury Project II (CJP2). The second phase, CJP2 ran from 2001 to roughly 2005 when the third phase of the project started. The second phase was housed at the University at Albany's School of Criminal Justice's Capital Punishment Research Initiative, part of the Hindelang Criminal Justice Research Center. The main tenants of the research project were to (1) to build and maintain a national archive for historical documents and data on the death penalty; (2) to plan and conduct basic and policy related research on capital punishment; and (3) to encourage scholarship, conduct graduate and undergraduate training, and disseminate scientifically grounded knowledge about the ultimate penal sanction.

The principal investigator of the Capital Jury Project was William J. Bowers (1935-2017). Professor Bowers received his Ph.D. from Columbia University in 1966. He was principal research scientist in the School of Criminal Justice, University at Albany, and formerly of the faculty of Northeastern University. For his research on capital punishment, in 2000 Dr. Bowers received the American Society of Criminology's August Vollmer Award for outstanding contributions in applied criminology.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Capital Jury Project records document the phases of the Capital Jury Research project. The collection contains materials ranging from 1941 to 2011, the bulk of the material is from 1991 to 2011. The collection is divided into parts that represent the individual phases of the project. Also represented in the collection are publications and research output from the findings as well as research on capital punishment in the United States. Much of the collection focuses on the first phase of the Capital Jury Project.

arrangement.head: Arrangement of the Collection
arrangement.p: The collection is arranged into eight series.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Preferred citation for this material is as follows:

Identification of specific item, series, box, folder, Capital Jury Project, 1941-2011. M.E. Grenander Department of Special Collections and Archives, University Libraries, University at Albany, State University of New York (hereafter referred to as the Capital Jury Project Records).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged into three sub-series - handwritten interviews with the jurors organized by state, administration of this phase of the study, and audio tapes of the interviews.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Phase I of the Capital Jury Project completed over 1,200 interviews with jurors in 353 capital trials in 15 states - Alabama, California, Florida, Georgia, Indiana, Kentucky, Indiana, Missouri, New Jersey, North Carolina, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, Texas, Tennessee and Virginia. These interviews chronicled the jurors' experiences and decision-making over the course of the trial, identified points at which various influences come into play, and revealed the ways in which jurors reached their final sentencing decision.

accessrestrict.head: Access
accessrestrict.p: To respect and fulfill the guidelines of the original National Science Foundation grant which supported the Capital Jury Project, sub-series 1.1 and 1.3 are currently closed to researchers. The materials will be open 75 years after the date of their respective creation. Sub-series 1.1 and 1.3 will be fully open in 2086 which accounts for undated material. Sub-series 1.2, which covers the administrative aspects of the CJP Phase I study, is open to researchers.

As an alternative to accessing the closed portions of this sub-series, researchers may also be able to access portions of aggregate data from the CJP study, but which are not part of the collection at the University at Albany, to test specific research questions. Please contact an archivist for more details.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This sub-series is arranged alphabetically by state and juror number.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series includes the juror interview instruments from the first phase of the Capital Jury Project.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: To respect and fulfill the guidelines of the original National Science Foundation grant which supported the Capital Jury Project, sub-series 1.1 is currently closed to researchers. The materials will be open 75 years after the date of their respective creation. Sub-series 1.1 will be fully open in 2086 which accounts for undated material.

As an alternative to accessing the closed portions of this sub-series, researchers may also be able to access portions of aggregate data from the CJP study, but which are not part of the collection at the University at Albany, to test specific research questions. Please contact an archivist for more details.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series includes the administrative materials from the first phase of the Capital Jury Project. It includes codebooks to the juror instruments, grant materials, and correspondence.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The project was continued by the Capital Jury Project III, a program of research on the decision-making of capital jurors. The states involved in this round of interviews were California, Delaware, Indiana, Louisiana, Missouri, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania, and Texas.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is divided into three subseries and includes handwritten interviews with the jurors organized by state, administrative files about this phase of the study, and files of the interviews (mainly transcripts) on discs.

accessrestrict.head: Access
accessrestrict.p: To respect and fulfill the guidelines of the original National Science Foundation grant which supported the Capital Jury Project, sub-series 3.1 and 3.3 are currently closed to researchers. The materials will be open 75 years after the date of their respective creation. Sub-series 3.1 and 3.3 will be fully open in 2086 which accounts for undated material. Sub-series 3.2, which covers the administrative aspects of the CJP Phase III study, is open to researchers.

As an alternative to accessing the closed portions of this sub-series, researchers may also be able to access portions of aggregate data from the CJP study, but which are not part of the collection at the University at Albany, to test specific research questions. Please contact an archivist for more details.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series consists of the state interview juror interviews of the third phase of the Capital Jury Project. The states involved in this round of interviews were California, Delaware, Indiana, Louisiana, Missouri, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania, and Texas. The name of the defendent was included in the file title without the respective, coded juror number.

accessrestrict.head: Access
accessrestrict.p: To respect and fulfill the guidelines of the original National Science Foundation grant which supported the Capital Jury Project, sub-series 3.1 is currently closed to researchers. The materials will be open 75 years after the date of their respective creation. Sub-series 3.1 will be fully open in 2086 which accounts for undated material.

As an alternative to accessing the closed portions of this sub-series, researchers may also be able to access portions of aggregate data from the CJP study, but which are not part of the collection at the University at Albany, to test specific research questions. Please contact an archivist for more details.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The administrative materials cover the period of time for the third phase of the Capital Jury Project. Much of the series materials are supplemental to the State Interview Summaries including informed consent from various institutions and the coding key to the juror instruments.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains the discs of the interviews with jurors and the interviewers. Much of the material is transcripts of trials and of juror interviews.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Principal Investigator for the Capital Jury Project was William J. Bowers (Ph.D., Columbia University, 1966). He was Principal Research Scientist in the School of Criminal Justice, University at Albany, and formerly at Northeastern University. For his research on capital punishment, in 2000 Dr. Bowers received the American Society of Criminology's August Vollmer Award for outstanding contributions in applied criminology. This series contains correspondence with other noted criminal justice scholars and activists, clemency applications, and general teaching materials including case transcripts while Dr. Bowers was a professor.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains Professor Bowers's personal correspondence with other noted Death Penalty scholars and activists.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains publications produced from the data collected in the Capital Jury Project as well as other publications.

did.unittitle: "A Comparison of the Work of Thorsten Sellin and Isaac Ehrlich on the Different Effect of Capital Punishment" by David C. Baldus and James W.L. Cole
"Executions Seen Selling into Violence" by Jean Dietz
"Arbitrariness and Discrimination Under Post-Furman Capital Statues" by William J. Bowers and Glenn Pierce
"Deciding Who Dies" by Stephen Gillers and Other Death Penalty Research
"Executions in America" - Draft by Henry Schwarzschild
"Executions in the United States, 1608-1987: The Espy File" by M. Watt Espy and John Ortiz Smykla
Female Offenders on Death Row, Articles by Victor L. Streib
Capital Punishment of Female Offenders Articles by Victor L. Streib
"The Adventures of an Abolitionist: 13 Years Against Executions" by Neil D. Hulkower
"Californians Want an Alternate to the Death Penalty" By William J. Bowers LA Times Op Ed Article
"The Death Penalty's Shaky Support" by William J. Bowers NY Times Article Including Correspondence and Related Research
"Massachusetts Voters Want an Alternative to the Death Penalty" by William J. Bowers
"New Yorkers Want an Alternative to the Death Penalty: Executive Summary of a New York Summary Conducted March 1-4, 1991" by Bowers, William J. and Vandiver, Margaret
"Nebraskans Want an Alternative to the Death Penalty" by William J. Bowers and Margaret Vandiver
"The Capital Juror Project: Background and Research Design"- CJP #1 by William J. Bowers and Magaret Vandiver
"Death Penalty Attitudes of Prospective Capital Jurors: The Indiana Voir Dire Project (A Preliminary Report)" - CJP #3 Joseph L. Hoffman
"Models of Decision Making in Capital Cases: Preliminary Findings" - CJP #2 by Marla Sandys
"Lottery od Life: Who Gets to Death Row" Transcript and Enid Harlow Correspondence
"Death Penalty Bill Raises Issues of Effects, Ethics" by Peter J. Howe and "The Governor's Quick Fix for Murders by William J. Bowers
"In Their Own Words: How Capital Jurors Explain Their Life or Death Sentencing Decisions" - CJP #6 by William J. Bowers and Margaret Vandiver
"In Their Own Words: How Capital Jurors Explain Their Life or Death Sentencing Decisions" - CJP #7 by William J. Bowers, Margaret Vandiver, and Patricia H. Dugan
"National Capital Jury Preliminary Results from South Carolina" CJP #10 by Theodore Eisenberg
"Jurors Response to the Defendants Testimony or Lack Thereof" - CJP #9 by William J. Bower and Heather S. MacAskill
"Tables for the Panel on the Capital Jury Project- In Progress: A Special Focus on Pre-Trial Publicity"- CJP #11 by Marla Sandys
"Proof Beyond a Reasonable Doubt: A Review of Jurors Thoughts"- CJP #8 by William J. Bowers and Andrea L. Waldo
"The Life or Death Decisions of Capital Jurors: Preliminary Findings from Kentucky"- CJP #4 by Marla Sandys
"Capital Punishment and the Myth [illusion] of Deterrence" Draft for the Globe by William J. Bowers
"Case Study: The Vanishing and Reappearing Penalty Trial"- CJP #15 by John Lamberth

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The subject files are some of the earilest material in the collection. The subject files came from the filing cabinets of Professor Bowers at Northeastern University. The material spans a great deal of interests and topics such ranging from Amnesty International to lethal injection to cameras in court.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Death Penalty News Clippings are clippings Professor Bowers collected for a number of years.
